message.content:
You are a senior editor at a WSJ/WP-caliber publication. I need you to settle the STRUCTURE and CONTENT of a one-page benchmark analysis, then hand me a concrete blueprint I will implement in HTML.

READ THE FILE FIRST: /tmp/claude-1000/-home-polaris-polaris-project/21e87760-8436-4090-870d-99ef2121882e/scratchpad/benchmark_index.html — it is the current draft. Read every section.

CONTEXT
- The product: "Deep Cove Research," a deep-research AI system (plans a question, searches the live web, writes a fully-cited report). It scores 0.5084 on DeepResearch Bench (RACE), ranking #3 of the systems shown, and is the highest-scoring OPEN-WEIGHT / self-hostable system (ahead of every closed frontier-lab model: Google, OpenAI, Anthropic, xAI; only ADORE (academic) and Tavily (closed) rank higher). Its differentiator is FAITHFULNESS: 90.3% citation accuracy on 84 verified citations — the most of any system measured.
- Audience: primarily the founder's boss (an executive) plus prospective enterprise evaluators. Register: serious, measured, WSJ/WP — no hype, each claim stated once, numbers carry the argument.
- The operator's verdict on the current draft: the SECTION ORDER is wrong and the CONTENT needs work. My draft's current order is: Hero → What it is → The field ranked (scoreboard) → Faithfulness chart → Two ways to build one (curated vs open internet) → Why it matters (foundation) → Inside the pipeline (funnel+architecture) → What it costs → The scored report → Methodology. The operator rejected this. Do not assume it is right.

YOUR JOB — deliver two things, concretely:
1. THE CANONICAL SECTION ORDER. Give the exact ordered list of sections for this page and this audience, with a one-line reason for each placement (why it must come where it does). If you keep some of my order, say so; where you move something, say why. Consider what an executive needs to believe, and in what sequence, to walk away convinced — and where a skeptic's objection must be answered before it forms. Name the single strongest opening and the right closer.
2. A CONTENT REVIEW, section by section. For each section: is the heading doing its job? Is any sentence confusing, hedged, or repetitive? Is anything redundant across sections (say what to cut)? Is anything MISSING that this audience will want? Where the prose is weak, give me the replacement line in WSJ voice. Be specific and quote the current text you're changing.

Hard constraints on content: every claim must stay factually precise (do not let me overclaim — e.g. we are NOT ahead of Tavily/ADORE; we ARE ahead of the frontier labs' closed models). Nothing self-deprecating. The sample report and the scoreboard data are fixed facts — you may reorder/reframe but not fabricate numbers.

Deliver your answer as a clean, implementable blueprint (ordered section list + per-section content edits). This is advisory to me — be decisive and opinionated; I will implement it. Your final message IS the blueprint (not a file).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
Analysis · Deep Research Benchmark

It outscores the frontier labs at deep research — and runs on open weights you host yourself.

A deep‑research system plans a question, searches the live web, and writes a report in which every claim is cited. Measured by RACE, the field’s standard quality score, Deep Cove Research places third among the systems tested here — ahead of the closed models from Google, OpenAI, Anthropic and xAI. It is the highest‑scoring system a company can run on its own hardware.

0.5084
Composite quality (RACE)
3rd overall · 1st among open weights
90.3%
Citation accuracy
claims that held up against live sources
84
Verified citations
the most of any system we measured

Scored on DeepResearch Bench under the RACE framework. Rows marked † below are the official 100‑task leaderboard; the rest are our own runs of each system’s latest model on one shared task.

What it is

An autonomous analyst, not a chatbot answer.

Ask a deep‑research system an open‑ended question and it does what a research analyst would. It breaks the question into parts, searches many sources, reads and extracts the evidence, weighs which sources to trust, reconciles the ones that disagree, and writes a long, structured report in which every claim carries a citation. The answer does not come from a model’s memory. It is assembled — search, verification, and synthesis, run end to end with no human in the loop.

The field, ranked

Where each system lands.

Every system here is scored by RACE, in which a GPT‑5.5 model grades the quality of each report. Deep Cove Research is the highest‑scoring self‑hostable system on the board, and it ranks ahead of every closed model from the major AI labs. Two systems edge it at the top.

# System Owner Country Model Source RACE
1 ADOREresearch submission academic — frontier LLM — 0.5265†
2 Tavily Researchagent framework Tavily United States model‑agnostic Closed 0.5244†
3 Deep Cove Researchself‑hosted Independent Self‑hosted Kimi‑K3 Open weights 0.5084
4 ClaudeResearch Anthropic United States Fable 5 Max Closed 0.5065
5 GeminiDeep Research Google United States 3.1 Pro Closed 0.4506
6 Kimi Researcheragentic Moonshot AI China Kimi Open weights 0.4460†
7 DoubaoDeepResearch ByteDance China Doubao Closed 0.4430†
8 GrokDeeperSearch xAI United States Grok Closed 0.4414
9 ChatGPTDeep Research OpenAI United States GPT‑5.6 Sol Pro Closed 0.4286

Deep Cove Research ranks third overall and first among systems a company can run itself. Only ADORE, an academic submission, and Tavily, a closed commercial framework, score higher. † Official DeepResearch Bench leaderboard, 100‑task average. Unmarked rows are our own runs of each system’s latest model on the shared benchmark task, scored on the same GPT‑5.5 harness; a full 100‑task submission for Deep Cove Research is in progress.

Faithfulness

Accuracy and volume rarely travel together.

A citation can fail two ways: the source may not support the claim, or the report may rest on too few citations to stand on. Most systems managed one and not the other. Deep Cove Research sits alone in the top‑right corner — high accuracy on a wide base of sources.

Grok’s accuracy was just as high, at 91% — but it rested on only 10 citations, where a single miss would move the figure nine points. Deep Cove Research’s 90.3% rests on 84 checked sources, a far sturdier base.

Two ways to build one

Curated dataset, or the open internet.

There are two ways to build a deep‑research system, and they solve different problems. One reads from a dataset that has already been collected, cleaned, and frozen. The other reads the live internet as it is — messy, contradictory, unbounded — and must find, judge, and verify the evidence itself on every run. The first is a useful tool. The second is a general research engine, and it is the one the frontier labs are racing to build.

Curated‑dataset systemOpen‑internet system
EvidenceA fixed corpus you pre‑assemble and cleanThe entire live web, in real time
CoverageOnly what was curated — blind to the restAny topic, any domain, including today
FreshnessFrozen — stale the moment it is builtAlways current
The hard partAssembling the corpus onceSearch, credibility, dedup & verification — solved live, every run
Generalizes?No — one dataset, one domainYes — one engine for every question
Who builds itTeams not yet able to handle the open webOpenAI, Google, Anthropic — and Deep Cove Research

A curated‑dataset system is a special case of an open‑internet one. A general engine can always be pointed at a fixed corpus; a curated system cannot be opened to the live web without first solving the harder problem. That is why the open‑internet path is the foundational one — and it is the one Deep Cove Research is built on.

Why it matters

Solve the open web, and everything sits on top.

A general deep‑research engine is not a single product. It is the layer beneath knowledge work. The same engine handles a due‑diligence request, a market scan, a literature review, a policy brief, or a standing risk monitor — with no new dataset to assemble for each one. Whoever can research the open internet reliably owns that layer.

Enterprise

Due diligence, market and competitive intelligence, regulatory and policy analysis — on demand, against current sources.

Knowledge & science

Literature reviews, evidence synthesis, technical scans — cited and verifiable, across any field.

Live monitoring

Standing questions answered against the web as it changes — something no frozen dataset can do.

This is the machine the frontier is building — and it is the machine Deep Cove Research is building, on open weights a company can run itself.

Inside the pipeline

From the open web to a verified report.

Every decision that shapes the report happens before a word is written — in which evidence is allowed in, and in how the writer is instructed. The report is never trimmed or rewritten afterward. That is what keeps it both faithful and coherent.

One real corpus, from retrieval to verified citation

997
evidence spans retrieved & grounded to quotes
~50%
set aside by the relevance gate as off‑topic
330
on‑topic, in‑scope sources kept for writing
84
citations verified against live URLs

The ten stages behind those numbers

01

Query planning & scope contract

The question is split into sub‑questions, and a scope contract is read straight from the prompt — the topics, source types, and constraints the answer must respect.

02

Multi‑source retrieval

The system searches academic journals, the open web, and official statistics agencies at once, in real time.

03

Grounded extraction

Evidence is saved as exact quotes, each tagged with its source. Nothing is paraphrased.

04

Relevance gate

A model judges every source against the actual question — an understanding of the question, not a keyword filter. It set aside roughly half of one real corpus as off‑topic.

05

Credibility tiering & corroboration

Sources are graded, duplicates merged, and the system counts how many independent sources back each finding.

06

Evidence packs

The graded, on‑topic evidence is organised into packs — the single input to writing.

07

Outline agent

An agent drafts the outline against the evidence, checks that every part of the question is covered, and revises.

08

Instructed writers

Writers draft sections in parallel, each tied to its own evidence. A precise prompt — not a post‑edit — makes them cite once, synthesise across sources, and write clean prose.

09

Faithfulness & citation check

Each claim is matched to its source, and the live page is scraped to confirm the source backs it. This drives the 90% citation accuracy.

10

Assembly

Sections are joined in order, the conclusion placed last, citations renumbered, and the report rendered with its sources.

The model itself — Kimi‑K3 — is a leading open‑weight system. It runs on your own hardware.

What it costs to run

Runs in‑house, at low cost.

Because the model is open‑weight, the whole pipeline runs on a company’s own servers — no per‑call API fees, and no data leaving the building.

~2.0M

Tokens to produce one full report, start to finish — search, writing, and verification.

~$1–2

Per report, at going open‑weight rates. No API fees. No data leaves the building.

The output

The report behind the numbers.

A representative scored report from Deep Cove Research — about 3,900 words, four evidence tables, and 37 tiered references, with every quantitative claim span‑grounded to a cited source.

Read the full report expand ↓

A literature review on the restructuring impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on the labor market

This report synthesizes the retrieved research evidence on the question above. It is organized as a coherent review: an introduction that frames the scope, thematic sections that group the evidence by sub-topic, a cross-study synthesis that surfaces where the findings agree and conflict, and a closing discussion of conclusions and open research gaps. Every quantitative claim is span-grounded to a cited source; claims that could not be verified against the underlying evidence were removed rather than paraphrased.

Introduction and Scope

The Fourth Industrial Revolution, a term popularized by Klaus Schwab in 2016, is characterized by a fusion of technologies that blurs the lines between the physical, digital, and biological spheres, distinguishing it from previous industrial revolutions through its unprecedented velocity, scope, and systems impact.[1][2] Artificial intelligence sits at the center of this transformation, acting as a key driver that orchestrates technologies such as robotics, automated vehicles, and real-time data analytics to redefine how work is performed across industries.[2] Global business investment in AI increased 13-fold from US$14.57 billion in 2013 to US$189 billion in 2023, with 35% of businesses already using AI in their operations and more than 50% intending to implement AI technologies.[3] This rapid diffusion has prompted predictions that STARA—smart technology, artificial intelligence, robotics, and algorithms—will replace a third of jobs that exist today, with forecasts that by 2025, 85 million jobs may be displaced while 97 million new roles may emerge.[4] Similarly, the World Economic Forum has predicted that AI adoption will make 75 million jobs redundant and create 133 million new ones worldwide by 2022, while economic analyses from PwC, IBM, Deloitte, and Gartner Research project that AI will increase global GDP by 15% by 2030, adding approximately $15.7 trillion.[5] A separate Future of Jobs report projected that 7.1 million jobs will be lost across 15 economic areas from 2015 to 2020, while 2.1 million additional jobs are expected to be created during the same period.[6] The net impact on labor demand depends on how these effects weigh against each other, and the authors find that the sharp slowdown of US wage bill growth over the last three decades reflects weaker-than-usual productivity growth combined with significant shifts in the task content of production against labor.[7] The scope of this disruption spans multiple sectors, reshaping employment through task automation, skill polarization, and industry-specific disruptions in healthcare, education, and creative industries, while also affecting white-collar professionals, gig workers, and freelancers.[3] Industrial robots already exceed 4 million units in operation, with installations steadily increasing due to falling costs and enhanced AI capabilities.[8] Acemoglu and Restrepo's task-based framework posits that automation generates a displacement effect, as capital takes over tasks previously performed by labor.[7]

Table 1. Headline employment forecasts cited in the literature

SourceDisplacedCreatedHorizon
STARA (smart tech, AI, robotics)85M97Mby 2025
World Economic Forum75M133Mby 2022
Future of Jobs (15 areas)7.1M2.1M2015–20
U.S. automation projection30% of jobs—by 2030

Figures as reported by each source; forecasts differ in scope and method and are not directly comparable.

Task-Based Frameworks and Displacement–Reinstatement

In this framework, production requires the completion of a range of tasks, each of which can be allocated to either capital or labor, and the resulting allocation determines the "task content of production".[7][9] Countervailing the displacement effect is the reinstatement effect, which arises when new labor-intensive tasks are created in which labor has a comparative advantage, directly increasing both the labor share and labor demand.[7][10][11] Historical examples abound: in the 19th century, technological developments generated employment for line workers, engineers, machinists, repairmen, conductors, managers, and financiers.[7] The pattern was particularly pronounced in manufacturing, where the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 1.1 percent per year, or about 30 percent cumulatively.[10][11] The baseline estimate of the elasticity of substitution between capital and labor at the industry level is 0.8, while firm-level elasticities are estimated between 0.4 and 0.7.[10][11] Complementary research by Autor, using U.S. data from 1940 through 2018, estimates that more than 60 percent of employment in 2018 was found in job titles that did not exist in 1940.[12] Between 1947 and 1987, wage bill per capita grew at 2.5 percent per year, largely explained by the productivity effect of 2.4 percent per year, while the displacement effect reduced labor demand at about 0.48 percent per year and the reinstatement effect increased it by 0.47 percent per year, yielding a near balance.[10][11]

Occupational Exposure and Susceptibility to AI

One framework proposes evaluating the potential impact of large language models by considering their relevance to the tasks workers perform, estimating that roughly 1.8% of jobs could have over half their tasks affected by LLMs with simple interfaces—jumping to just over 46% when accounting for complementary software.[13] AI's potential impact extends beyond routine tasks to nonroutine ones such as diagnosing health conditions, programming computers, and tracking flight routes, though some affected occupations are augmented rather than replaced.[14] Across OECD countries, the occupations most exposed to AI tend to be white-collar roles such as IT professionals, business professionals, managers, and science and engineering professionals; occupations requiring manual skills are the least exposed.[15] In Korea, 32.2% of firms reported that AI increased the kinds of skills required and 38.3% reported an increase in the level of skills required, while 95.5% reported no workforce changes at the department level and 56.5% said AI replaced specific tasks rather than eliminating positions.[15]

Empirical Evidence on Employment and Labor Demand

In occupations where computer use is high, a one standard deviation increase in AI exposure was associated with 5.7 percentage points higher employment growth.[16] Instrumental variable estimates showed AI increased high-skilled employment by 0.376% and medium-skilled employment by 0.397%, while low-skilled employment declined by 0.003%.[17] One study estimated AI will reduce 90:10 wage inequality but will not affect the top 1%.[18] Research on generative AI's short-term effects in an online labor market found it reduced overall demand for workers.[19] Another analysis observed that AI's impact has been modest so far, echoing past innovation cycles.[21] Jobs for AI and Machine Learning specialists have been rising at a compounding rate of 32% per year between 2016 and 2023.[22]

Table 2. Estimated employment effect of AI, by skill level

Study / settingHighMediumLow
Cross-country, IV estimate+0.376%+0.397%−0.003%
China, per one-unit rise in AI+0.063%+0.001%−0.001%

Consistent direction: AI raises high- and medium-skilled employment while slightly reducing low-skilled employment.

Productivity Effects of Generative AI

In a preregistered experiment, Noy et al. (2023) assigned writing tasks to 453 professionals and randomly exposed half to ChatGPT, finding average time taken decreased by 40% and output quality rose by 18%, while reducing inequality between workers.[24] A systematic review of 194 peer-reviewed articles (2011–2025) concludes AI functions as a general-purpose technology with uneven, context-dependent effects.[25] An ILO research brief across seven countries finds productivity gains are real albeit often unverified and uneven, and worker-reported time savings of a few percent have not yet translated into higher measured output, earnings, or employment.[26] Among workers using generative AI at least once in the previous month, 31.9% spent an hour or more per workday using it.[27] Information services showed the largest share of work hours using generative AI at 14.0% and the highest time savings at 2.6%.[27] A standard model estimated self-reported time savings translate to a 1.1% increase in aggregate productivity.[27]

Table 3. Generative-AI use intensity and time saved

SegmentTypeGenAI shareTime saved
Computer & mathematicsoccupation~12%2.5%
Personal serviceoccupation1.3%0.4%
Information servicesindustry14.0%2.6%
Leisure & accommodationindustry2.3%0.6%

A 10-point rise in time spent using generative AI is associated with a 1.7-point rise in time saved as a share of hours worked.[27]

Wage Inequality, Skill Demand, and Labor Polarization

The share of the employed population with AI skills remains small—at most 0.3% in OECD countries on average—but is growing rapidly, with nearly half the AI workforce in the top two deciles of the labour earnings distribution.[28] A one standard deviation increase in AI exposure is associated with a 0.4 percentage point increase in wage growth, driven by occupations requiring high software familiarity.[28][29] These patterns imply AI has the potential to exacerbate labor market polarization, as benefits accrue disproportionately to workers already at the upper end of the skill and income distribution.[29][30] Job vacancies for AI roles have increased seven-fold since 2012, and jobs requiring AI skills have grown 3.5 times faster than overall vacancies.[31] Notably, generative AI applications benefit the least experienced workers most, with less experienced programmers completing tasks over 50% faster—suggesting AI may under certain conditions decrease performance inequality.[28]

Policy Implications and Frameworks for Managing Disruption

Sectoral reallocation can be costly because of potential mismatch between the skills of unemployed workers and those required by firms.[34] Cazzaniga and others (2024) assume AI shocks would reduce the labor share by 5.5 percentage points based on the historical UK change between 1980 and 2014.[34] A significantly larger share of employment is in occupations with high augmentation potential across regions, ranging from 10.2 percent in Sub-Saharan Africa to 16.1 percent in Southeastern Asia and the Pacific.[35] Online platform workers in the US regularly report median earnings of roughly $2 to $3 per hour, well below the federal minimum wage.[35]

Cross-Study Synthesis and Conclusions

The cross-study evidence reveals a field still in its earliest stages, with convergent acknowledgment that current findings are fragmented and preliminary.[36][37] Early large-scale evidence is consistent with the hypothesis that the AI revolution is beginning to have a significant and disproportionate impact on entry-level workers in the American labor market.[37] Some research found a decline in job postings began in 2022, prior to ChatGPT, corresponding better to rising interest rates than to LLMs, and Frank et al. (2026) found similar results.[36] Important questions about AI's effects on the labor market remain unanswered, and there is a need for more research and better data.[37][36][35]

Limitations

The corpus exhibits tier-distribution gaps, with only 6% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 21% falling into T6, while a further 15% remain UNKNOWN. No contradictions were detected across paired sources, so no cross-source disagreement is asserted. The telemetry block does not surface a date range, limiting characterization of the evidence horizon.

Table 4. Evidence base by source-quality tier

TierDescriptionShare
T1primary studies6%
T6lower-authority sources21%
UNKNOWNcould not be categorized15%

Remaining share spans intermediate tiers (T2–T5).

References

[1] The Fourth Industrial Revolution: what it means and how to respond — weforum.org (T4)

[2] Fourth Industrial Revolution — Wikipedia (T4)

[3] Artificial intelligence and technological unemployment — sciencedirect.com (T4)

[4] The Fourth Industrial Revolution – Smart Technology, AI — PMC9301265 (T1)

[5] Unlocking the value of AI in HR management — doi.org/10.1016/j.hrmr.2022.100899 (T1)

[6] Fourth Industrial Revolution between Knowledge Management and Digital Humanities — doi.org/10.3390/info13060292 (T4)

[7] Automation and New Tasks: How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor — aeaweb.org (T1)

[8] Robots, AI, and automation: the 4th industrial revolution is here — tomorrow.city (T4)

[9] Automation and New Tasks — doi.org/10.1257/jep.33.2.3 (T1)

[10] How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor — shapingwork.mit.edu (T4)

[11] How Technology Displaces and Reinstates Labor — concetticontrastivi.org (T4)

[12] The Labor Market Impacts of Technological Change — nber.org w30074 (T4)

[13] GPTs are GPTs: Labor market impact potential of LLMs (T1)

[14] The potential impact of AI innovations on US occupations — PMC11421150 (T1)

[15] Artificial Intelligence and the Labour Market in Korea — OECD (T3)

[16] AI and Employment: New Cross-Country Evidence — PMC9127971 (T1)

[17] How AI affects the labour force employment — PMC10907740 (T1)

[18] The Impact of AI on the Labor Market — michaelwebb.co (T4)

[19] Short-Term Effects of Generative AI — profiles.wustl.edu (T2)

[20] 59 AI Job Statistics — nu.edu (UNKNOWN)

[21] AI and Jobs: Limited Disruption So Far — morganstanley.com (T4)

[22] The AI Revolution in Labor — japksu.com (T4)

[23] AI, Task Changes in Jobs, and Worker Reallocation — iza.org dp17554 (UNKNOWN)

[24] Experimental evidence on productivity effects of generative AI — pubmed 37440646 (T1)

[25] AI and Economic Development: A Systematic Review — doi.org/10.1007/s13132-026-03385-w (T2)

[26] Research Brief: impact of GenAI on jobs — ilo.org (T3)

[27] The Impact of Generative AI on Work Productivity — St. Louis Fed (T3)

[28] AI, job quality and inclusiveness — OECD Employment Outlook 2023 (T3)

[29] The Occupational Impact of AI: Labor, Skills, Polarization — econbiz.de (T2)

[30] The economic impact of AI on employment and income disparities — econstor.eu (T4)

[31] Business students' perceptions of AI's impact on the labor market — doi.org/10.3846/jbem.2025.24349 (T1)

[32] Automation and Industry 4.0 in production engineering — doi.org/10.7862/rm.2025.14 (T4)

[33] Occupations and Inequality: Theoretical Perspectives — doi.org/10.1007/s11577-020-00685-0 (T4)

[34] Broadening the Gains from Generative AI — IMF elibrary (T3)

[35] Mind the AI Divide — United Nations / ILO (T3)

[36] Research on AI and the labor market is still in the first inning — hamiltonproject.org (T2)

[37] AI and Labor Markets: What We Know and Don't Know — digitaleconomy.stanford.edu (T2)

Methodology. The scoreboard combines two sources. Rows without a dagger are our own runs of each system’s latest model on one shared DeepResearch Bench task — a literature review on AI and the labor market — all through the same harness, with a GPT‑5.5 model scoring quality against the benchmark’s reference report. Rows marked † are taken from the official DeepResearch Bench leaderboard, a 100‑task average under the same RACE framework. The two are close in scale but cover different task sets, so the ranking is indicative; a full 100‑task submission for Deep Cove Research is in progress. Citation figures — accuracy (the share of checked citations that held up) and verified citations (the number that held up) — come from our own runs: each claim was matched to its source, the live page scraped, and an automated judge checked whether the page supported the claim.

content.text:
I've read the full draft. Here is the blueprint.

---

# THE VERDICT IN BRIEF

Your top four sections are in the right order. The back half is wrong, and the failure is specific: after Faithfulness — the evidentiary peak — the draft runs two consecutive abstract strategy essays, then a ten-box architecture grid, and only **then**, at position 8, tells the executive what this costs. An executive reads five screens. In your draft, the reader who stops at screen five has seen the strategy sermon but never the price, never "no data leaves the building." Cost is not an appendix; it is the second half of your headline claim ("runs on open weights you host yourself"). Move it up. Merge the two strategy sections — they are one argument told twice. Close with the artifact.

# 1. THE CANONICAL SECTION ORDER

1. **Hero** — *keep at 1.* The strongest possible opening is the claim itself plus the three stats; nothing else on this page earns attention faster.
2. **What it is** — *keep at 2, cut by half.* The category must be defined before the scoreboard means anything; but the hero lede already half-defines it, so this section earns its place only if it stops repeating the lede.
3. **The field, ranked** — *keep at 3.* The exec will not accept a single framing sentence until the table has been seen; proof before argument, always.
4. **Faithfulness** — *keep at 4.* It answers the objection the scoreboard just created ("a quality grade from an LLM judge — can I trust what these things write?") and it is your differentiator; it must sit flush against the table.
5. **What it costs** — *moved up from 8.* The moment the exec believes the numbers, the next question is "what does it cost me, and where does my data go?" Answer it before they scroll into anything abstract. Faithfulness + cost, back to back, is also your complete answer to "why not just buy Tavily?": the two systems above you are closed; this one you own.
6. **The harder problem** — *merge sections 5 and 6 of your draft ("Two ways to build one" + "Why it matters") into one section, here.* They are a single argument — the open web is the hard, general case; whoever solves it owns the layer beneath knowledge work — and running them separately makes the page repeat itself ("the frontier labs are racing to build" / "this is the machine the frontier is building"). Placed here, it is the strategic close of the executive half of the page: the case for why this asset compounds.
7. **Inside the pipeline** — *stays roughly here (was 7).* This is for the technical evaluator, and it substantiates the 90.3% with mechanism; it belongs after the business case, not inside it.
8. **The scored report** — *keep as closer.* The right last word is the product itself: after every claim, here is the artifact, read it. Nothing you could write is more convincing than letting them open it.
9. **Methodology footer** — *keep last.* Standard placement; it is the receipts drawer, not a section.

**Strongest opening:** the hero as concept — the frontier-labs claim plus the 0.5084 / 90.3% / 84 stat row. **Right closer:** the expandable report. The page's silent argument is "we will show you everything," and ending on the raw artifact is what makes that argument land.

# 2. CONTENT REVIEW, SECTION BY SECTION

## §1 Hero

**Headline.** "It outscores the frontier labs…" — the pronoun "It" has no antecedent; that is Apple-keynote styling on a page whose register is analysis. WSJ names the subject or leads with the finding. Replace with:

> **"Ahead of the frontier labs at deep research. Running on hardware you own."**

Two sentences, no dangling pronoun, and it puts both halves of the claim in the headline.

**Lede.** Currently does three jobs (defines the category, states the rank, states the open-weight claim) and duplicates §2. Tighten to:

> "Deep Cove Research plans a question, searches the live web, and writes a fully cited report. On RACE, the quality score of DeepResearch Bench, it places third of the nine systems tested — ahead of the closed models from Google, OpenAI, Anthropic and xAI — and first among systems a company can run on its own machines."

**Precision flag:** "the field's standard quality score" is a shade of overclaim — RACE is *a* public benchmark's framework, not an anointed standard. "The quality score of DeepResearch Bench, the field's public benchmark" is defensible; "the field's standard" is not quite.

**Byline.** It currently pre-explains the dagger, which the table caption and footer also do — three tellings of one caveat. Cut the byline to one line: *"Scored on DeepResearch Bench under the RACE framework. Methodology below."* Keep the full dagger explanation at the table, where the objection actually forms.

**Stat cards:** all three are right. "the most of any system we measured" on card 3 is the correct hedge — keep it exactly.

## §2 What it is

Heading is good — "An autonomous analyst, not a chatbot answer" is the best heading on the page. Keep it.

The body re-lists what the hero lede just said. Cut the inventory, keep the two things the hero *didn't* say — judgment and autonomy:

> "Ask it an open-ended question and it does what a research analyst would: breaks the question into parts, weighs which sources to trust, reconciles the ones that disagree, and writes a structured report in which every claim carries a citation. The answer is not recalled from a model's memory. It is assembled — searched, verified, and written end to end, with no human in the loop."

Six lines, no repetition, and the section now earns its slot.

## §3 The field, ranked

**Heading.** "Where each system lands." is generic — it wastes the one heading that could carry your best number. Replace:

> **"Third of nine. First among systems you can own."**

**Intro paragraph** is honest and well-built; "Two systems edge it at the top" is exactly the right pre-emption. Sharpen it by naming what they are, since that is your defense: *"Two systems edge it at the top — an academic submission and a closed commercial framework. Neither can be self-hosted."* (Verify the ADORE claim before shipping the "neither" — if ADORE's status is unknown, write "an academic research submission and a closed commercial framework.")

**The Country column must go.** Your own row reads Country: "Self-hosted," which is not a country, and the column's only real work — flagging the two Chinese systems — is done more cleanly by the Owner column, which already says Moonshot AI and ByteDance. Dropping it removes clutter and removes a question you don't want asked ("wait, what country *is* this from?"). The Source column (Open weights / Closed) is the one doing the strategic work; keep it.

**The strongest credibility line on this page is currently missing.** The self-run rows were graded by a GPT-5.5 judge — OpenAI's model — and OpenAI's own system finished ninth. If that is accurate, say it in the caption:

> "The judge is a GPT-5.5 model. It placed the system built on OpenAI's own frontier model last."

That single sentence dissolves the "you graded your own homework" objection better than any methodology paragraph. Verify it holds (same judge across all unmarked rows), then use it.

**Caption:** keep the dagger note and "a full 100-task submission is in progress" here — this is where the skeptic needs it — and delete the duplicate "in progress" line from the methodology footer.

## §4 Faithfulness

**Heading** — "Accuracy and volume rarely travel together." — is excellent. Do not touch it.

**Intro:** "A citation can fail two ways: the source may not support the claim, or the report may rest on too few citations to stand on." The second failure isn't a citation failing; the logic wobbles. Replace:

> "A report can fail its reader two ways: citations that do not support the claims, or so few checkable citations that its accuracy means little. Most systems solved one problem or the other. Deep Cove Research sits alone in the top-right corner — high accuracy on the widest base of checked sources."

**The Grok caption is the best-edited paragraph in the draft.** "…only 10 citations, where a single miss would move the figure nine points" — keep it verbatim.

**Missing:** the chart shows five systems; the table shows nine. A careful reader will ask where Tavily and ADORE went. Add one clause to the caption: *"Citation checks cover the five systems we ran ourselves; leaderboard-only entries publish no citation data."*

## §5 What it costs (moved up)

**Heading.** "Runs in-house, at low cost." tells; the numbers should. Replace:

> **"A dollar or two a report, on your own servers."**

**Precision flag:** "Per report, at going open-weight rates" sits awkwardly next to "No per-call API fees" — if it's self-hosted there are no rates. What you mean is marginal compute priced at open-weight market rates. Say that: *"Marginal compute per report, priced at going open-weight rates — a ceiling, not a fee."*

**Cut the duplicate:** "no data leaving the building" appears in the intro *and* bolded in the card. Keep it once, in the card, bolded — it is the single phrase the enterprise evaluator will repeat upstairs.

**Move here** the orphaned line from the pipeline section ("The model itself — Kimi-K3 — is a leading open-weight system. It runs on your own hardware."), reworded: *"The engine is Kimi-K3, a leading open-weight model. Nothing in the pipeline calls a closed API."* (Confirm that last clause is true before using it; if the judge or any stage calls out, drop it.)

## §6 The harder problem (merged from your 5 + 6)

**Structure of the merged section:** opening graf (two ways to build) → comparison table → the "special case" paragraph → the "layer beneath knowledge work" paragraph → the three use-case cards. Cut the old §6 closing cap ("This is the machine the frontier is building…") entirely — the table's last row already says it.

**Eyebrow:** "The harder problem." **Heading:** keep your §6 heading — "Solve the open web, and everything sits on top." — it's the better of the two and now titles the whole argument.

**One line must change.** The table row "Who builds it: *Teams not yet able to handle the open web*" is a sneer, and it's the only sentence on the page that sounds like a competitor wrote it. Replace with: *"Vertical tools scoped to one domain."* State your side's strength; never the other side's inadequacy — that's the WSJ line between confident and defensive.

**Keep verbatim:** "A curated-dataset system is a special case of an open-internet one" and "Whoever can research the open internet reliably owns that layer." Those two sentences are the section.

## §7 Inside the pipeline

**The funnel math doesn't survive arithmetic — this is the one factual landmine on the page.** The boxes read 997 → "~50% set aside" → 330 → 84. Any numerate reader computes 997 × 50% ≈ 500, not 330, and now doubts every other number here. Two clean fixes; pick whichever matches the real telemetry: (a) relabel the middle step with the true total attrition ("two of every three spans set aside — off-topic, duplicate, or out of scope"), or (b) add the intermediate step (relevance gate → ~500, dedup/tiering → 330). Do not ship the current version. While you're there, step 04's prose ("roughly half of one real corpus") must match whatever the funnel ends up saying.

**Consistency:** step 09 says "the 90% citation accuracy" — the page says 90.3% everywhere else. One number, everywhere.

**The intro paragraph** ("Every decision that shapes the report happens before a word is written… never trimmed or rewritten afterward") is distinctive and true to the system's actual design philosophy. Keep it — it is the one technical claim an executive will remember.

The ten stages are fine for the evaluator audience. If you want them tighter, stages 06 and 10 are the expendable prose.

## §8 The scored report

Heading and framing are right. Add one sentence before the expander that converts the report's own Limitations section from a liability into evidence:

> "Note the report's closing section: it states what its evidence base could not support. The system reports what it cannot verify — that discipline is where the 90.3% comes from."

That is not self-deprecation; it is the faithfulness claim demonstrated rather than asserted, and it ties the closer back to the differentiator.

## §9 Methodology footer

Keep the full paragraph, minus the "100-task submission is in progress" sentence (now lives only at the table caption). **Add the one thing missing:** the date of the runs. Models change monthly; an undated benchmark reads as stale on arrival. One clause: "Runs conducted [month year]."

# 3. THE REDUNDANCY LEDGER (cut list, one place each)

| Claim | Appears | Keep it in |
|---|---|---|
| Dagger / two-source scoreboard | hero byline, table caption, footer | table caption (short) + footer (full); gut the byline |
| "100-task submission in progress" | table caption, footer | table caption only |
| "No data leaves the building" | cost intro, cost card | cost card only |
| Category definition (plans/searches/cites) | hero lede, §2 | one line in hero; §2 carries judgment + autonomy |
| "The frontier labs are racing to build this" | §5 prose, §5 table row, §6 cap | the table row only |
| Kimi-K3 open-weight line | pipeline tail, cost section | cost section |

# 4. VERIFY-BEFORE-SHIP FLAGS

1. **Funnel arithmetic** (997/50%/330) — must reconcile before publication.
2. **"GPT-5.5 judge placed OpenAI's system last"** — powerful; use only if the same judge scored all unmarked rows.
3. **"Neither top system can be self-hosted"** — confirm ADORE's status; soften to description if unknown.
4. **"Nothing in the pipeline calls a closed API"** — use only if literally true.
5. **"The field's standard quality score"** — soften to "the field's public benchmark."

Everything else in the draft is factually careful — the third-place framing, the "we measured" hedges, and the Grok-sample-size caption are exactly right and should survive every future edit.
